chapterTitle: The Coffee That Knew Where to Go
glitchName: The Willemstad Café (or: How Luisana's Compass Works)
imageCaption: Luisana stands in a sun-drenched café that shouldn't exist, holding a cup of coffee that steams with the specific aroma of somewhere she's never been but always dreamed of, while her family materializes around her like they were always meant to be there.
text:
The café appeared between one blink and the next.

Luisana had been standing in the impossible Funchal-Lisbon-Unknown airport, watching her family try to figure out how to board a flight that didn't technically exist, when she'd caught a scent on the air. Not from the airport. From somewhere else. Somewhere warm. Somewhere that smelled like excellent coffee and salt air and yellow doors.

She'd followed her nose, which had always been a more reliable navigation system than any app, and walked through what she thought was a bathroom door. It wasn't a bathroom door. It was an entirely different reality.

The café was small, painted in shades of blue that looked like they'd been stolen directly from the Caribbean Sea. The tiles on the floor were geometric—diamonds and stars in warm terracotta and white. Through the window, she could see colonial architecture, narrow streets, and a harbor that her late-night real estate browsing had shown her approximately seven hundred times.

Willemstad. Curaçao.

It was impossible. It was also, impossibly, a café.

"Can I help you?" the owner asked. He was older, with kind eyes and the sort of accent that suggested he'd lived in exactly one place his entire life and had no plans to change that arrangement.

"I—" Luisana started, then stopped. Her journalist brain was trying to ask questions that made logical sense. Her heart was asking something different. "Do you know a family named Gomez?"

The man smiled like she'd asked the most sensible question in the world. "Not yet. But I will. That's how these things work, yes? First the café arrives. Then the family arrives. Then the story starts to make sense."

Luisana's pencil was already in her hand, moving across the notepad she'd been carrying. *Day 15. The café found me. Or I found it. Or it was waiting.* She paused, then added: *Yellow doors. Azure tiles. The kind of place you dream about without knowing you're dreaming.*

That's when Aaliyah walked through the bathroom door.

"Mom?" Her daughter took in the scene with the rapid-fire assessment of someone who'd spent two weeks having spatial geography become purely theoretical. "Are we in—"

"Curaçao," Luisana confirmed. "I think. A café in Curaçao. Or a glitch-café that is currently located in Curaçao. The distinction seems increasingly theoretical."

Aaliyah's eyes went wide. She started drawing immediately, her pencil capturing the tiles, the geometric precision of it, the way the blue matched something she'd clearly seen in her mother's late-night browsing sessions. "This is new," she said. "The glitches have never been this specific before. They've been random. Chaotic. But this is—"

"Intentional," Luisana finished. She turned to the café owner. "The glitches. Are they your doing?"

The man laughed—a warm, genuine sound. "My doing? No. But I've been here a long time. Long enough to notice when reality gets thin in a place. And I've been expecting visitors. People who don't need a plan. People who show up because they're supposed to, not because they scheduled it."

Anthony appeared in the doorway next, moving with the cautious hope of someone who'd learned that doors in airports led to increasingly improbable locations. Behind him, Elijah tumbled through still holding his notebook, Sully at his heels.

Sully stopped dead in the middle of the café and barked—short, sharp, almost confused. Then he padded directly to the café owner and sat at his feet like they'd known each other for years.

"Sully knows," Elijah announced. "He says this is a good place. He says the glitch here is different. It's not trying to break anything. It's trying to build something."

Anthony looked at his wife, and Luisana watched something settle in his expression—recognition, maybe. Or confirmation of something he'd known all along.

"The pegboard," he said softly. He pulled out the roll of license plates, and there, glowing gently among the collection, were two new additions. One was the Curaçao flag. The other was a plate that simply read: *HOME.*

Luisana felt her eyes get hot in the way they did before tears, which was ridiculous because they weren't there yet, they were still in the middle of an impossible journey, and yet—

"Coffee?" the café owner offered, already pouring. "I've been holding this pot for you for exactly the right amount of time."

Luisana took the cup. It was warm. It was perfect. It tasted like someplace she'd never been but had somehow always known how to find.

"How did you know we were coming?" she asked.

The man smiled. "The glitches don't break things at random," he said. "They break in the shape of what needs to happen. They glitch toward resonance. They glitch toward home." He paused, looking at the family gathered in his impossible café. "You are the resonance. The world is breaking so that you can put it back together. It's been doing that since the moment you stopped following a plan."

Aaliyah was writing furiously, her pencil barely keeping up with the implications cascading through her head. Elijah was petting Sully, who seemed to have appointed himself official café greeter. Anthony was staring at the license plate that said *HOME* like it was the most important thing he'd ever collected.

And Luisana—Luisana was drinking coffee that tasted like every late-night dream, every real estate listing viewed at 2 AM, every conversation with Anthony about yellow doors and spontaneous lives.

She was home.

Well. Almost.
